TW Framework

Root middleware.tw Documentation

Is document ka goal: root middleware.tw me rule-based “modern middleware” explain karna, jahan developer sirf rules likhta hai aur framework request ko wahi par allow/block karta hai.

High-level behavior

  • Request sabse pehle middleware.tw rules se pass hoti hai.
  • Jis rule me condition match aur violation trigger hota hai, framework usi point par response return kar deta hai.
  • Is case me API route ya page handler hit nahi hota.
  • Developer ko redirect/rewritestatus/body define hota hai.

Quick start

Project root me middleware.tw create karo, phir rules add karo. Server restart ke baad rules apply ho jayenge.

tw serve --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8787

Order matters: rules top-to-bottom evaluate hote hain. Pehle wale matching rule ka effect pehle lagega.

File location

middleware.tw project root me hota hai (same level as tw.config).

Agar multiple candidates hain, framework root-level ko prefer karta hai. Recommended: sirf root me hi rakho.

DSL structure

Ab recommended format:

rule "name" {
  match "/api/**"
  methods ["GET","POST"]

  # conditions / checks
  user_agent { ... }
  origin { ... }
  path { ... }
  auth { ... }
  rate_limit { ... }

  # response (what to return if blocked)
  response { ... }
}

Legacy use { ... } blocks abhi bhi exist kar sakte hain, lekin “modern rules” ke liye rule { ... } prefer karo.

Supported blocks

Block Kab use karein Typical output
user_agent { ... } Bots/scrapers ko allow/block (UA patterns se) 403 Forbidden / 404 Not Found
path { ... } Scanner paths, extensions, traversal, slug sanity 404 Not Found
origin { ... } API origin/referer allowlist 403 Access Denied (often JSON)
auth { ... } Cookie + HS256 JWT verification (admin/api protection) 401 Unauthorized
rate_limit { ... } Per-IP (ya IP+path bucket) token bucket rate limiting 429 Too Many Requests
response { ... } Block hone par exactly kya return karna hai Text/HTML/JSON response

Rule keys

  • match: path pattern (examples: "/**", "/api/**", "/admin/**").
  • methods: list of allowed methods (optional). Example: ["GET","POST"].

Agar methods set hai, rule sirf un methods par apply hoga. Otherwise all methods.

Response block

response { ... } me aap decide karte ho ki block hone par exactly kya return hoga.

response {
  status 403
  html "<h1>403 Forbidden</h1>"
  header "Content-Security-Policy" "default-src 'none'"
}

Supported keys:

  • status (number)
  • text (string)
  • html (string)
  • content_type (string)
  • json { ... } (object)
  • header "Name" "Value"
  • cookie "name" "value"

Agar aap json { ... } use karte ho to default content-type JSON ho jata hai (unless explicitly override).

Examples

Blocked bots (403)

rule "blocked-bots" {
  match "/**"

  user_agent {
    allow ["googlebot", "bingbot", "gptbot"]
    block ["curl/", "wget/", "python-requests", "scrapy", "ahrefsbot"]
    empty_is_blocked true
  }

  response {
    status 403
    html "<!doctype html><h1>403 Forbidden</h1>"
  }
}

Is rule me allow-list pehle check hoti hai. Allowed bot match hua to block patterns ignore honge.

Scanner / malicious paths (404)

rule "scanner-paths" {
  match "/**"

  path {
    prefixes ["/wp-admin", "/wp-login", "/.env", "/.git"]
    contains ["/phpmyadmin", "/xmlrpc"]
    extensions [".php", ".sql", ".bak", ".env"]
    single_segment_max 100
    deny_traversal true
    deny_null_bytes true
  }

  response {
    status 404
    text "Not Found"
  }
}

Ye “security by obscurity” nahi, practical noise reduction hai: scanners ko 404 do, app logic tak mat jaane do.

Admin protection (JWT cookie) (401)

rule "admin-auth" {
  match "/admin/**"

  auth {
    cookie "admin_token"
    jwt_secret_env "JWT_SECRET"
  }

  response {
    status 401
    text "Unauthorized"
  }
}

Isme redirect nahi hai. Developer apne UI flow ke hisaab se 401 handle kar sakta hai.

API origin allowlist (403 JSON)

rule "api-origin" {
  match "/api/**"
  methods ["GET","POST","PUT","PATCH","DELETE"]

  origin {
    allow ["https://twmods.in", "http://localhost:3000"]
    require true
    allow_referer true
  }

  response {
    status 403
    json { error "Access Denied" }
  }
}

Origin missing ho aur require true ho to request block ho jayegi.

Rate limit (429)

rule "api-rate-limit" {
  match "/api/**"

  rate_limit {
    requests 60
    window 60
    identity "path"
    bucket_segments 2
  }

  response {
    status 429
    json { error "Too many requests" }
    header "Retry-After" "60"
  }
}

Current implementation in-memory hai (per process). Testing/dev ke liye perfect. Multi-instance production me shared store (Redis) better hota.

Evaluation order (important)

  • Rules file top-to-bottom evaluate hoti hai.
  • Har rule me pehle match (and optional methods) check hota hai.
  • Then conditions run: path, user_agent, origin, auth, rate_limit.
  • Jaise hi kisi check ka violation hua, framework turant response return kar deta hai.

Isliye generic block rules (e.g. “block all bots”) ko bahut upar mat rakho, warna legit traffic bhi block ho sakta hai.

Testing

UA test

curl -i -H "User-Agent: curl/8.0" http://127.0.0.1:8787/

Origin test (API)

curl -i -H "Origin: https://evil.example" http://127.0.0.1:8787/api/users

Rate limit test

for i in $(seq 1 80); do
  curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" http://127.0.0.1:8787/api/users
done

Notes / limitations

  • Rule engine intentionally “simple” hai: patterns + allowlists + fixed responses.
  • JWT verification HS256 model follow karta hai (secret-based). Secret ko env me rakho.
  • Origin check strong security guarantee nahi hota (mobile apps / curl can forge headers). Ye mainly browser-originated requests filter karta hai.
  • Rate limit per-process memory me hai. Multiple servers/instances me exact global limit nahi rahega.

Is design ka main benefit: request ko early stop karke app/API ko unnecessary hit se bachana.

Document: root middleware.tw rule system.
